text: G20 is an absolutely hilarious diehard
spoof that playfully makes fun of all
the ridiculous tropes of modern
Hollywood, featuring an outofshape woman
in her late 50s who has to take on an
army of evil crypto bros while battling
sexism on her own side, aided by her
teenage tech genius daughter to stop an
Australian dude from crashing the
world's financial markets. And honestly,
I haven't laughed so much in years.
Watching an almost 60-year-old Viola
Davis beating up a 250 lb meathead with
a frying pan really shows that Amazon
have come to terms with how ridiculous
this girl boss stuff is and decided to
fully embrace the comedy potential of
modern
Hollywoods. Wait, what? This is a
serious movie? Really? Oh my god.
Good lord. Talk about backing the wrong
horse. If you ever wondered why Jennifer
Sulki was fired as head of Amazon
Studios, well, G20 is a perfect example.
A hilariously earnest attempt to push
the girl boss trope to its logical
conclusion by making the protagonist not
just an asskicking gun toing Mary Sue,
but literally the leader of the free
world as well. wasting a talented cast
with stilted hackne dialogue that dumps
so much exposition it should have been
prosecuted by the EPA and a story that
reads like a laundry list of current-day
buzzword issues that Hollywood writers
barely understand but sound really cool
like AI deep fakes global digital
currencies crypto scams and internet
misinformation and it's all rammed home
with amateur hour direction and shoddy
CGI that makes what was probably a
pretty expensive production look
laughably cheap and unbelievable. In
short, G20 is slop, pure and simple. A
movie that embodies all the worst
aspects of direct to streaming projects
like this, probably designed to be
watched in the background while you
catch up on your online shopping on
Amazon, presumably. And while I'm
talking about that, did you know that
you can make big savings on your online
purchases with Amazon's daily deals,
which track online retailers to make
sure that you always get the best price
possible.
Anyway, the plots. So, Viola Davis stars
as President Sutson, a former soldier

online

today and not government corruption,
poor infrastructure, droughts, terrorist
terist attacks, civil wars, population
displacement, diseases, or shortage of
chemical pesticides.

I don't think that's true. For some

tropes of modern writing are present and
correct with this one. There's the
sweaty middle-aged white guy that
constantly talks down to Sutton and
exists mostly to be wrong about
everything. The rebellious teenage
daughter who's so smart she can
literally hack the White House security
system and escape the Secret Service to
go drinking with her friends in
Washington. Should have made her the
main character if I'm honest. and a
recent addition to the Hollywood writing
playbook. The vaguely right-wing online
influencer villain spreading
disinformation for his own personal
benefits. Ultimately, G20 is a movie
that feels out of time and out of place.
Made before the cultural winds had
shifted and probably banking on certain
events in America last year turning out
quite differently. It's a film that's
laughable when it tries to be serious,
boring when it tries to be gripping, and
probably not worth 2 hours of your time.
